title: System hardening compliance within the SWIFT secure zone
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the SWIFT CSP programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with SWIFT CSP control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for 1.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that SWIFT CSP 1.1 (SWIFT environment protection) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.34.5: System hardening compliance within the SWIFT secure zone.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.34.5' running on catalogue-defined data sources (see UC detailedImplementation), archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'System hardening compliance within the SWIFT secure zone' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper SWIFT CSP control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the SWIFT CSP control objective.

dataSources: SWIFT Alliance/Lite2 transaction logs, operator session audit trails, network infrastructure logs, endpoint security logs, privileged access management, change management records, vulnerability scan outputs, multi-factor authentication logs
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (742), Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (4295), Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757), Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621) (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=os sourcetype=package OR index=wineventlog sourcetype="WinEventLog:Security" host="SWIFT-*" earliest=-24h
| stats latest(version) as ver by package, host
| lookup swift_hardening_baseline.csv package OUTPUT min_version
| where ver<min_version
description: Produces SWIFT Customer Security Programme evidence for system hardening compliance within the swift secure zone ahead of independent assessment.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for SWIFT CSP compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes os, wineventlog and sourcetypes WinEventLog:Security, package. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Segment SWIFT CSP evidence into dedicated indexes with strict role-based access. (2) Join operators to HR screening and vendor risk lookups refreshed weekly. (3) Validate MFA and PAM sourcetypes are CIM-mapped for Authentication models. (4) Retain evidence for at least 13 months aligned to attestation cycles. Indexes required: `index=os`, `index=wineventlog`.
visualization: Table (operators), Single value (sessions), Bar chart (CSCF controls). Key fields: `host`, `package`, `ver`.
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.dest | sort - count
dataModelAcceleration: Enable acceleration for Authentication (CIM data model).
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `os`, `wineventlog` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `swift_hardening_baseline.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('swift_hardening_baseline.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `1.1`. The control objective is: "Evidence that SWIFT CSP 1.1 (SWIFT environment protection) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.34.5: System hardening compliance within the SWIFT secure zone." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for Microsoft Windows (Splunkbase app ID 742), landing events in `os`, `wineventlog` under `package`, `WinEventLog:Security`. Security channel alone produces 2000-8000 events per domain-joined host per day depending on audit policy (~2-8 GB/host/month). Domain controllers generate 10x more.

Configure the input through Splunk_TA_windows/local/inputs.conf on each Universal Forwarder. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[WinEventLog://Security]
disabled = 0
index = windows
renderXml = true
current_only = 0
evt_resolve_ad_obj = 1
checkpointInterval = 5
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `EventCode, Account_Name, Account_Domain, Logon_Type, Source_Network_Address, Workstation_Name, Process_Name, TargetUserName, Subject_Account_Name`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=os earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Four distinct operational considerations shape this detection's rollout:


• **Hint 1:** Segment SWIFT CSP evidence into dedicated indexes with strict role-based access. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 2:** Join operators to HR screening and vendor risk lookups refreshed weekly. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 3:** Validate MFA and PAM sourcetypes are CIM-mapped for Authentication models. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 4:** Retain evidence for at least 13 months aligned to attestation cycles. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "System hardening compliance within the SWIFT secure zone," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the SWIFT CSP control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `swift_hardening_baseline.csv` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`min_version`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `package`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `swift_hardening_baseline.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval package = lower(package)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 24 hours.

**CIM / accelerated variant:** The detection also has an accelerated variant using `| tstats` against the CIM `Authentication` datamodel:

```spl
| tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication by Authentication.dest | sort - count
```

When to prefer this variant: once the underlying datamodel is accelerated (verify in Settings > Data Models > `Authentication` > status=100%), this query runs 10-50x faster than the raw variant and can feasibly cover multi-month windows for audit reporting. When to avoid: if your field extractions are app-specific and the CIM tagging is incomplete, the `tstats` variant will silently under-count. Always compare raw vs accelerated counts on a known day before swapping the production schedule. Maintain both variants in the UC — the raw is the primary for accuracy, the accelerated is the performance optimisation for dashboarding.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.34.5: System hardening compliance within the SWIFT secure zone]
cron_schedule = 0 1 * * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -24h
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.34.5,reg=SWIFT_CSP
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=os sourcetype=package earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `host`, `package`, `ver`. Run:

```spl
index=os sourcetype=package earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table host, package, ver
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `swift_hardening_baseline.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup swift_hardening_baseline.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.34.5: SWIFT CSP evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (`operators`):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Single-value KPI (`sessions`):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'SWIFT CSP current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.

**Panel 3 — Distribution (`CSCF controls`):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**SWIFT CSP-specific failure modes:**

• CSCF (Customer Security Controls Framework) Mandatory vs Advisory: CSCF distinguishes mandatory (must implement) from advisory. Detections must tag which category each finding belongs to — advisory findings don't prevent attestation.

• Architecture Type A vs B: SWIFT defines two architecture types. Detections must know which architecture applies; the same control has different implementation expectations per architecture.

• Swift CSP attestation cadence: annual attestation on July 31 anchoring point. Detections producing attestation evidence must align to this annual cycle; quarterly evidence is over-frequent, daily is too granular.

• Controls shifted mandatory (framework drift): SWIFT moves controls from advisory to mandatory each year. Reconcile UC-to-framework mapping annually as of the latest CSCF version.
grandmaExplanation: We help you show SWIFT security programme expectations are met for your payment zone, operators, and controls before the independent check.
